title: Dog Not Responding to Name: Causes, Triage, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog ignoring their name? Learn about hearing loss, cognitive decline, and ear health. Low urgency triage guide for concerned pet owners.
content_html:
TL;DR: A dog not responding to their name may have hearing loss, an ear infection, or a behavioral issue. While usually not an emergency, you should schedule a vet visit to rule out medical conditions, especially if you notice head tilting or dizziness.

Why is my dog not responding to their name?

  • Sensory issues like hearing loss, which is common in senior dogs.
  • Physical obstructions such as ear infections or wax buildup.
  • Neurological issues that cause a breakdown in communication between sound and the brain.
  • Behavioral changes where the dog has become desensitized to the sound of their name.

Is it an emergency if my dog stops responding to their name?

  • Urgency Level: Low. In the absence of collapse, extreme lethargy, or seizures, this is typically not a medical emergency.
  • Schedule a veterinary visit to determine if the cause is a treatable ear infection or a more complex neurological condition.
  • Seek an appointment more promptly if your dog also appears dizzy or is tilting their head.

How can a photo or video help determine why my dog isn't responding?

  • Provide a clear, close-up photo of your dog's ear canals to check for visible redness, swelling, or dark discharge.
  • Share a video of you calling your dog while they are looking away to help a professional assess if the dog is truly failing to hear or choosing to ignore the sound.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A dog not responding to its name may be attributed to several underlying causes, including cognitive dysfunction syndrome (CDS), particularly in geriatric dogs, endocrine disorders such as Cushing's disease, pain, sensory decline (especially age-related hearing loss or presbycusis affecting mid to high frequencies), or diseases affecting the central nervous system (e.g., tumors) or circulation (e.g., anemia, hypertension). Furthermore, bilateral deafness can occur following general anesthesia for dental or ear cleaning. A detailed history, physical and neurologic examination, and diagnostic tests are required to exclude potential medical causes.

Chapter: Neurology, Geriatrics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1569)

faqs.question: Can dogs suddenly go deaf?
faqs.answer: While gradual hearing loss is more common with age, sudden deafness can occur due to severe infection, trauma, or certain medications. A veterinary exam is necessary to find the cause.

faqs.question: Could my dog just be ignoring me?
faqs.answer: Yes, behavioral 'selective hearing' is possible, especially if the dog is distracted or hasn't had consistent training. However, it is safest to rule out medical hearing loss first.

faqs.question: Is hearing loss in dogs treatable?
faqs.answer: If the cause is an ear infection or a physical blockage, hearing often returns once the condition is treated. Age-related hearing loss is usually permanent but manageable.
